planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Kaleidoscope develops its most vivid yellow-to-orange foliage color in full sun — in partial shade the leaf color is more yellow-green and less intense.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Kaleidoscope is the most celebrated and widely planted variegated abelia cultivar — named for its remarkable seasonal foliage color changes that shift through a wide range of colors through the growing season. The new spring growth emerges brilliant golden-yellow to chartreuse; through summer the leaves display yellow margins on green centers; in fall the foliage transitions to vivid orange, red, and burgundy-red — an essentially continuous foliage display that provides more sustained ornamental interest than almost any other compact landscape shrub. The small, white to pale-pink tubular flowers bloom prolifically from spring through frost, adding light fragrance and excellent butterfly and hummingbird value throughout the season. The arching, well-branched habit stays compact at 2.5–3.5 feet tall and wide with minimal pruning. Kaleidoscope won the award of the American Nursery & Landscape Association and has become one of the most popular landscape shrubs introduced in the early twenty-first century. Like all abelias, it is extremely tough — tolerating drought, clay soil, heat, humidity, and partial shade with minimal care. Essentially pest-free; deer-resistant. Prune lightly after spring bloom to shape; remove any all-green reverted shoots immediately to prevent them from overtaking the variegated growth.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Rose Creek is one of the most cold-hardy abelia selections and also one of the most compact — excellent for small foundation plantings and tight spaces.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Rose Creek is a compact, cold-hardy abelia cultivar selected for its tight, mounding growth habit (2–3 feet tall and wide), exceptional cold hardiness (reliably hardy to zone 5 with protection, zone 6 without), and profuse white flower production from spring through frost. The white, tubular flowers are produced on arching stems in clusters and have the characteristic sweet, light fragrance of abelia flowers that attracts butterflies and hummingbirds. The small, dark-green foliage turns attractive bronze-reddish in fall and winter, providing seasonal color change interest. Rose Creek is somewhat more upright and compact than standard Abelia × grandiflora and its smaller stature makes it appropriate for tight foundation positions and smaller garden spaces where standard abelia would become too large. Like all abelias, Rose Creek is remarkably adaptable — tolerating clay soils, drought, heat, humidity, and partial shade that challenge many flowering shrubs. Minimal pruning is needed — remove any winter-damaged stems in late winter and lightly shape immediately after spring bloom if size control is needed. Plant 3–5 for foundation borders or in masses for sweeping effect.

planting.note: Plant bare-root divisions in fall (September–October in zone 8) in well-drained, deeply prepared, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; set the eyes (growth buds) no deeper than 1–2 inches below the soil surface — deeper planting is the most common cause of peonies failing to bloom.

directions: Plant bare-root divisions in fall in well-drained, deeply prepared, fertile soil in full sun; Sarah Bernhardt is one of the most famous and most widely grown peony cultivars in the world — introduced by Léon Lemoine of Nancy, France, in 1906 and named for the legendary French actress, it remains the best-selling peony in American commerce after more than a century in cultivation. The enormous, fully double, light apple-blossom pink flowers (6–8 inches across) with a sweet, intensely classic peony fragrance represent the idealized peony — the standard against which other peonies are measured for form, color, and fragrance. The petals are arranged in a lush, layered rosette pattern of exceptional fullness and symmetry. Sarah Bernhardt blooms in late May in zone 8, and the cut flowers last 7–10 days in arrangements. Peonies are long-lived perennials — Sarah Bernhardt specimens planted decades ago continue to bloom reliably without division. The primary zone 9 limitation is insufficient winter chilling — peonies require 500–1000 chill hours to bloom reliably, and in the warmest parts of zone 9 and all of zone 10, the winter chilling requirement is not met. In zone 8 and cooler parts of zone 9 (north Louisiana, central Texas), peonies can succeed. Plant with only 1–2 inches of soil over the crown — deeper planting is the primary cause of peony failure to bloom.

common_name: Sun Gold Cherry Tomato (Orange Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Sun Gold is an indeterminate cherry tomato that produces continuously from June through frost in zone 9 when kept well-staked and fertilized.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Sun Gold is widely considered the finest-tasting cherry tomato ever developed and consistently ranks as the top-rated cherry tomato in taste trials — a Japanese hybrid producing small (3/4-inch), round to slightly elongated, deep orange fruits with exceptional sweetness, complex fruity-tropical flavor notes, and almost no acidity. The fruity, candy-sweet flavor of Sun Gold is distinctly different from other cherry tomatoes — a tropical, almost perfumed sweetness that makes it irresistible eaten directly from the vine. In zone 9, Sun Gold can be expected to produce continuously from transplanting in February through June and again from September through frost in the fall — the summer heat above 95°F temporarily stops fruit set but the plant remains and produces heavily once temperatures moderate. The vigorous, indeterminate vine requires a sturdy cage or staking to 5–8 feet and sets fruit in prolific clusters (trusses) of 10–20 tomatoes simultaneously. Sun Gold is a patented F1 hybrid — seed cannot be saved true-to-type. The attractive orange fruit color and extraordinary flavor make it one of the most popular and most widely recommended garden tomatoes.

common_name: Yellow Pear Tomato (Heirloom Yellow Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun at or just before the last frost date; Yellow Pear is an heirloom cultivar with true-to-type seed that can be saved from year to year.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Yellow Pear is an heirloom cherry tomato with a history extending back to at least the 1800s — producing clusters of small, bright-yellow, pear-shaped fruits with a distinctive teardrop shape and mild, low-acid, sweet flavor. The fruit shape and bright yellow color make Yellow Pear one of the most visually distinctive tomatoes in the garden, and it is a consistent favorite at farmers markets and in mixed salads where its cheerful yellow color and unusual shape create visual interest. The low-acid flavor profile makes it approachable for children and adults who find standard tomatoes too acidic. The indeterminate vine grows vigorously to 5–8 feet and sets fruit prolifically throughout the growing season — an established Yellow Pear plant produces hundreds of fruits over the course of the season. Seed can be saved from non-hybrid heirloom Yellow Pear true-to-type for future seasons — select fruit from the most vigorous and productive plants. In zone 9, Yellow Pear performs well in the spring window (February–June) and fall window (September–frost) but stalls during the peak summer heat above 95°F. The low-acid taste makes it excellent for children's snacking, fresh salads, and pasta dishes where a mild tomato flavor is preferred.
